turns.content: What does PER actually measure and why do some analysts think it's flawed?

Player Efficiency Rating (PER) is John Hollinger’s attempt to compress a box-score stat line into a single per-minute number normalized so league average is 15. Conceptually, it’s “rate of box-score production” with weights for positive events (made shots, free throws, assists, rebounds, blocks, steals) and penalties for negative events (missed shots, turnovers, personal fouls), then adjusted for pace and minutes. What it actually measures well: how much a player stuffs the traditional box score per minute, with a scoring bias. Because it’s per-minute, it tends to like high-usage players who generate a lot of events, and it can make low-minute bench players look extreme if they have spiky production. Why people call it flawed: 1) Box-score only: it ignores defense that doesn’t show up as blocks/steals, screening, spacing gravity, hockey assists, and scheme roles. 2) Weighting choices are subjective and not transparently tied to points or wins; some weights “double count” or overcredit certain events. 3) It’s not context-aware: teammate quality, role, opponent, and lineup effects aren’t modeled. 4) It’s sensitive to usage and efficiency tradeoffs; it can like high-volume scorers even if their overall impact is more complicated. 5) The “replacement” baseline is not explicit; it’s normalized to league average, not to wins added. So PER is more like “box-score productivity per minute” than “overall value.” It’s not useless, but it’s a blunt instrument.
Can you walk me through the basic math structure? Not every coefficient, but the flow from box score to the final PER.

Sure—high level, PER is built in layers: 1) Compute “unadjusted” productivity per minute: - Start with a linear combination of box-score events. Think: + credit: FGM (with extra for 3PM), FTM, assists, rebounds (split OREB/DREB), steals, blocks - debit: missed FG (FGA - FGM), missed FT (FTA - FTM), turnovers, personal fouls - The weights aren’t exactly “points” weights; they’re tuned to Hollinger’s view of marginal value of each event, and some are scaled by league factors (like assist value depends on league assist rate). 2) Adjust for team pace: - Faster-paced teams create more possessions, so raw per-minute box score can be inflated. PER uses a pace adjustment so players on fast teams aren’t automatically boosted relative to slow teams. 3) Normalize to league average: - After computing a per-minute efficiency number, it’s scaled so the league average PER is exactly 15. That makes it easy to interpret historically within an era, but it also means the scale is relative, not absolute “wins.” 4) Apply season/league constants: - There are constants derived from league totals each season to keep the metric stable and ensure the normalization works. So the flow is: weighted box score per minute → pace adjustment → league normalization (average = 15). The key is that the box-score weights and league factors are doing most of the “modeling,” and those choices drive the criticisms.
People say PER overvalues scoring. Is that just because points are easy to count, or is it structurally baked in?

It’s structurally baked in. Even though PER includes lots of non-scoring events, the scoring components dominate variance because: - Scoring events are frequent and high magnitude (FGM, FGA, FTM, FTA happen a lot). - Usage correlates with many credited events (assists, drawn fouls, offensive rebounds can correlate with being on-ball or near the action). - The penalty for missed shots is present, but high-usage scorers can still come out ahead if they produce enough makes/FTs relative to misses and turnovers. Also, PER’s assist credit can amplify the value of primary creators, and the rebound/defense weights often aren’t enough to offset the sheer volume of scoring opportunities. So a player with “quiet” elite defense and low-usage offense can look mediocre in PER.
How does PER treat efficiency? Like, if two players score 25 a game but one is way more efficient, does PER capture that?

Yes, partially. PER penalizes missed shots and missed free throws, so higher true shooting tends to help. But it’s not a true “points per possession” model; it’s a linear box-score model. Two caveats: 1) Shot mix and spacing value: PER doesn’t know whether a shot came late clock, was self-created, or created by teammates. It just sees makes/misses and threes. 2) Turnovers and fouls: Efficiency isn’t only shooting; if the efficient scorer turns it over a lot or racks up fouls, PER will penalize those. So PER will generally rank the more efficient scorer higher if the box-score differences show up in makes vs misses and FT%. But it won’t capture off-ball efficiency contributions like spacing that improves team offense without direct box-score events.
Okay, pivot to Win Shares. What are Win Shares trying to do that PER isn’t?

Win Shares (WS), originally popularized in basketball by Justin Kubatko and inspired by baseball’s win shares concept, is trying to allocate team wins to players based on their contributions. That’s fundamentally different from PER’s “rate of box-score production.” Core idea: - Start with team performance (offensive efficiency and defensive efficiency, in points per possession terms). - Convert team efficiency into an estimate of team wins (using a Pythagorean expectation-like relationship). - Then apportion those wins to players based on their offensive and defensive box-score contributions. So WS is explicitly anchored to wins: the unit is “wins.” PER is unitless and normalized.
But Win Shares are still box-score based, right? How do they infer defense?

Correct—Win Shares relies heavily on box-score, plus team defense context. Offensive Win Shares: - More directly box-score driven: shooting efficiency, usage, assists, offensive rebounds, turnovers, etc., mapped into “marginal offense” and then into wins. Defensive Win Shares: - This is the more controversial part. It starts from team defensive performance (points allowed per possession, adjusted for pace) and then allocates credit across players using proxies like: - Defensive rebounds - Steals - Blocks - Personal fouls (sometimes as a debit) - Minutes played (a big driver) Because individual defense is hard to capture in the box score, WS tends to give a lot of defensive win shares to players on elite defensive teams who play heavy minutes, even if their individual defensive impact is ambiguous. Conversely, a great defender on a mediocre defensive team can look under-credited.
So is the main critique that WS is too team-context dependent?

That’s a major critique, yes. Specifically: 1) Defensive WS inherits team defense: - If the team is elite defensively, there’s a lot of “defensive win” value to allocate, and minutes become a big allocator. That can make certain players look better defensively than impact metrics would suggest. 2) Role dependence: - Some roles (e.g., primary rim protection, point-of-attack containment, switch versatility) don’t translate cleanly to steals/blocks/DRB. WS can misallocate among teammates. 3) Interaction effects: - Defense is highly synergistic; two players can be “more than the sum of parts.” A linear allocation struggles with that. 4) Offense is also context-laden: - Teammates influence shot quality and assist opportunities. WS uses team-level efficiency baselines, but the attribution to players is still imperfect. So WS is good for “roughly who was on the floor for winning-level production and filled the box score efficiently,” but it’s not a clean measure of isolated individual impact.
Can you compare WS, WS/48, and why people prefer per-minute or per-possession versions?

WS is cumulative: total estimated wins contributed over the season (or career). It’s heavily influenced by minutes. A durable player with good-but-not-elite per-minute impact can accumulate lots of WS. WS/48 is a rate version: win shares per 48 minutes. It tries to answer “how much win contribution per minute,” controlling for playing time. League average is usually around .100 WS/48 (varies by era and calculation updates), with elite seasons pushing .250+. Why rate stats are used: - To separate “how good when playing” from “how much played.” - To compare players with different minutes. - To identify efficiency/impact independent of availability. Why cumulative stats are used: - Wins are accumulated over minutes; playing more matters. - For awards or season value, total contribution often matters. A good practice is to look at both: WS (value) and WS/48 (quality), then sanity check with other metrics.
Where do BPM and VORP fit into this? Are they “better” than PER/WS?

BPM (Box Plus/Minus) and VORP (Value Over Replacement Player) are also box-score derived, but they’re designed to approximate plus-minus impact rather than “efficiency rating” or “win allocation.” BPM: - A regression-based model that uses box-score stats (and position estimates) to predict a player’s on-court plus-minus per 100 possessions relative to league average. - It includes separate offensive and defensive components (OBPM/DBPM), though DBPM is still noisy because defense isn’t well captured in box score. VORP: - Converts BPM into “value over replacement” and scales by minutes, producing a cumulative number. It’s similar in spirit to WS but anchored to a replacement baseline and the plus-minus framework. Are they better? - They’re often more aligned with impact metrics than PER because the target is “plus-minus,” not “box-score productivity.” - But they remain box-score models; they can still misread defense, roles, and certain player types (especially low-box-score, high-impact defenders or screen/spacing hubs). So “better” depends on what you want: PER is descriptive of box-score rate production; WS tries to allocate wins; BPM/VORP tries to approximate impact.
I’ve heard of RAPM and RPM and EPM. How do those differ conceptually from box-score metrics?

Those are primarily plus-minus based impact metrics, which start from lineup data: how the team performs with a player on the court versus off, adjusting for teammates and opponents. RAPM (Regularized Adjusted Plus-Minus): - Uses possession-level or stint-level scoring margin data. - Adjusts for who else is on the floor (teammates and opponents). - “Regularized” means it uses ridge regression (or similar) to stabilize estimates because raw APM is extremely noisy. RPM (Real Plus-Minus, ESPN) historically blended: - A plus-minus backbone with priors from box score and tracking data (details changed over time and aren’t fully public). EPM (Estimated Plus-Minus): - A modern approach combining adjusted plus-minus with box-score and tracking-informed priors, aiming for better stability and predictive power. - Public versions exist (e.g., DunksAndThrees EPM), with more methodological transparency than some proprietary metrics. Conceptual difference: - Box-score metrics assume value comes from recorded events. - Impact metrics infer value from changes in team scoring margin while accounting for context. Tradeoff: - Impact metrics can capture “invisible” contributions (spacing, screening, deterrence) but have noise and require large samples and good models. - Box-score metrics are stable and interpretable but can miss key parts of the game.
If RAPM is so noisy, why do people treat it like gold?

Because it’s closer to the thing we actually care about: “How does the scoreboard move when this player plays, controlling for context?” Even if noisy, it’s aiming at causal-ish impact. Also: - Over large samples (multi-year), RAPM becomes much more stable. - It tends to validate certain archetypes box-score metrics underrate (elite defenders, connectors). - It’s harder to “game” with stat-padding because it’s not directly built from box-score events. But it’s not perfect. RAPM can struggle with: - Collinearity: players who share most minutes (e.g., a locked-in starting unit) are hard to separate. - Role and lineup constraints: coaches deploy players in certain contexts; the model may attribute context to the player. - Non-stationarity: a player’s skill changes over time; multi-year RAPM averages over that. So it’s “gold” as an anchor, not as an oracle.
Let’s get more specific. Suppose a player has mediocre PER but great on/off. What are the usual explanations?

Common explanations: 1) Defense not in box score: - The player’s value is in positioning, deterrence, communication, contest quality, or scheme execution—none of which PER sees. 2) Offensive “connective tissue”: - Quick decisions, secondary creation, swing passes, screens that create advantages, spacing gravity. These show up indirectly in team efficiency but not as individual box-score events. 3) Lineup fit: - The player amplifies teammates’ strengths or covers weaknesses. On/off can capture synergy; PER won’t. 4) Usage distortion: - PER tends to reward high-usage production. A low-usage player who makes the offense hum might not accumulate the events PER wants. 5) Sample/context in on/off: - Sometimes it’s noise: opponent bench lineups, matchup patterns, or a player sharing minutes with the best teammates. That’s why adjusted plus-minus is preferred to raw on/off.
Conversely, high PER but mediocre impact?

That happens too: 1) Box-score stuffing without possession value: - Some events are less valuable than they look. Example: grabbing uncontested defensive rebounds in a scheme where guards are tasked to rebound; it pads counts but may not change outcomes much. 2) Defensive liabilities: - PER doesn’t punish poor defense unless it manifests as fouls or lack of steals/blocks. A high-usage scorer can have a big PER while being targeted defensively. 3) Turnover/shot quality externalities: - PER counts misses and turnovers, but it doesn’t capture how a player’s style affects teammates’ shot quality or offensive structure. 4) Minutes and rotation effects: - Per-minute box-score dominance can come in lineups that don’t translate to winning against top competition; impact metrics against stronger opponents can look worse.
How should I interpret PER historically? Like comparing 2004 to 2024.

With caution. PER is normalized each season so league average is 15, which helps cross-era comparisons superficially. But the underlying environment changes: - Pace, three-point rate, shot distribution, offensive efficiency, foul rates. - Stat-keeping changes and strategic shifts (e.g., assists patterns, rebound distribution). Because the weights were designed in an older context and then updated via seasonal constants, PER’s relative scale is comparable in the sense “how far above average in that season,” but it’s not necessarily comparable in meaning. A 25 PER season in one era might come from different skill mixtures than a 25 PER season in another. If you want cross-era comparisons, it’s better to triangulate: - PER (relative box-score dominance) - WS/48 or BPM (box-score impact proxies) - era-adjusted rates (TS% relative to league, rTS; rebound%, assist% relative to position norms) - and where possible, multi-year impact estimates (hard across eras).
You mentioned “assist value depends on league assist rate.” That sounds weird. Explain.

In Hollinger’s framework, an assist is valuable because it usually leads to a made basket, but the “marginal” value of an assist depends on how common assists are and how they correlate with scoring in that environment. So PER uses league-level factors to scale certain components so the metric stays calibrated to league totals. If the league has a high assist rate, the model’s baseline assumption about what an assist represents can shift, and the coefficient adjusts accordingly. This is one reason PER is hard to “intuit” from first principles: some weights are not fixed basketball constants, they’re season-dependent calibrations. That helps with normalization but makes the metric less transparent and potentially less stable conceptually.
If I wanted a metric that is “closest to points,” what should I look at?

For “closest to points,” you want something in points per possession or points added terms. Options: - True Shooting Percentage (TS%): scoring efficiency, but not volume by itself. - Points per 100 possessions + TS% together: volume and efficiency. - Offensive Rating (individual): estimates points produced per 100 possessions used; but individual ORtg has assumptions and can be noisy. - Estimated plus-minus offense component (like OEPM): directly tied to scoring margin impact. - For a more accounting-style approach: “points added” models that estimate how many points above average a player contributes via shooting, turnovers, offensive rebounds, and playmaking (often from play-by-play or tracking). If you want something interpretable and not too model-heavy: combine TS% (or rTS), usage, turnover rate, assist rate, and on-court offensive rating.
Let’s talk about Win Shares more. How does WS convert “marginal points” into wins?

At a high level: 1) Estimate marginal points: points produced above a baseline (often tied to league average efficiency). 2) Convert marginal points to marginal wins using a “points per win” factor. The “points per win” relationship depends on the scoring environment. In higher-scoring eras, it generally takes more points of differential to translate to one win, because variance and baseline scoring levels differ. WS uses a season-specific conversion based on league data. Then: - Offensive Win Shares come from marginal offense relative to average. - Defensive Win Shares come from marginal defense relative to average. - Total WS = OWS + DWS. This is why WS is sensitive to team context: marginal defense is computed from team defensive rating, then distributed.
Is WS additive across players? Like, do team win shares sum to team wins?

Approximately, yes—that’s a design goal. Team WS is constructed to align with team wins (often very close, though rounding and some implementation details can create small discrepancies). That additivity is part of its appeal: it’s an allocation system. But additivity doesn’t guarantee correctness at the player level—it just means the accounting balances. If the allocation rule misattributes defense among teammates, the team total can still match wins while individual splits are off.
How does WS handle bench units and garbage time? Does it matter?

WS is mostly box-score and minutes-based with team efficiency context, so it doesn’t explicitly separate “high-leverage” minutes from lower-leverage minutes. If a player racks up strong box-score production in low-stakes segments, WS will still count it. Impact metrics based on possession data also usually don’t weight leverage unless explicitly designed to (most aren’t). Some analysts will look at: - performance in “rotation minutes” (e.g., first three quarters, closer lineups), - or use lineup/role filters, but standard WS doesn’t. In practice, over a large season, the leverage effect is smaller than people think for most players, but for certain deep bench players it can matter.
PER also doesn’t care about leverage, right?

Right. PER is blind to game state. It treats every minute and every box-score event the same. That’s consistent with its purpose (a rate stat), but it doesn’t answer “who helps you win close games,” except indirectly through correlations.
If I’m building my own metric, what’s the simplest “good” approach without getting into heavy regression?

A simple but defensible approach is a “points above average” box-score model grounded in possessions: 1) Offense: - Shooting: estimate points above average from TS% relative to league on the player’s scoring possessions (FGA and FTA converted to shooting possessions). - Turnovers: subtract points relative to average possession value. - Offensive rebounds: add value for extending possessions (roughly league points per possession times extra possessions). - Passing: either credit assists with a conservative value (like a fraction of expected points created) or avoid direct assist credit and rely on on/off if you have it. 2) Defense: - If you want to avoid noisy box-score defense, either: a) use team defensive rating while player is on court (needs play-by-play), or b) keep defense minimal (steals, blocks, defensive rebounds) and admit it’s incomplete. 3) Convert points to wins: - Use a points-per-win factor from league data (often ~30 points per win, varies). This will be less “fancy” than BPM/EPM, but it will be interpretable and you’ll know exactly what assumptions you’re making.
That sounds like you’re reinventing components of BPM. What’s the key difference?

BPM is fitted to predict plus-minus using historical data, so the coefficients emerge from regression (with constraints and positional adjustments). Your “simple points model” would be more axiomatic: you choose values based on possession economics. Pros of regression (BPM-like): - Empirically tuned to match scoreboard impact. - Can implicitly learn that some box-score events correlate with impact more than their naive point value. Cons: - Coefficients can be non-intuitive. - Model can encode era-specific quirks or biases in the data. - Defense remains hard; regression may “attribute” defensive impact to correlated offensive stats or position. Axiomatic model pros: - Transparent, interpretable, easier to reason about. - Easier to adjust philosophically. Cons: - Might miss interaction effects and subtle correlations that regression captures.
Let’s dig into “positional adjustments.” Why do these metrics care about position?

Because the same box-score line can imply different impact depending on role and typical positional responsibilities. Example: - 8 rebounds for a guard might be rarer and possibly more valuable (or at least more indicative of activity/impact) than 8 rebounds for a center, because centers are expected to rebound. - 6 assists from a big might be more indicative of playmaking value than 6 assists from a point guard, depending on context. BPM includes a position estimate (based on height, rebounds, assists, etc.) and adjusts coefficients accordingly. This helps prevent the model from overrating players who compile “out-of-position” stats that correlate with impact, and it tries to compare players more fairly across roles. PER doesn’t do much positional adjustment; it’s more “one formula fits all,” which can overrate certain big-man stat profiles.
So is PER especially kind to bigs?

Historically, yes. PER tends to like: - high FG% finishers, - strong rebounders, - shot blockers, - players who avoid turnovers. Those traits map well to traditional big roles and show up strongly in the box score. Meanwhile, perimeter defense, off-ball offense, and spacing can be undercounted. Modern bigs who space the floor may get some benefit from 3PM, but the core structure still often favors interior box-score production.
Give me an example archetype PER underrates.

A low-usage, high-minute perimeter stopper who: - doesn’t get many steals/blocks, - doesn’t rebound much, - takes few shots, - makes quick passes that don’t become assists, - sets effective screens and provides spacing. Their on-court defensive impact and offensive connectivity can be huge, but PER will see “not many credited events” and rate them as average or below.
And an archetype PER overrates?

A high-usage bench scorer who: - takes lots of shots, - gets to the line, - racks up points and a few assists, - but is a defensive negative and/or has high turnovers. If their shooting efficiency is decent, PER can be very high even if their overall impact is mixed. Another is a rebound-heavy big with limited defensive mobility: rebounds and blocks can push PER up even if team defense suffers in ways PER can’t see.
How do analysts validate metrics? Like, what’s the standard test?

There are a few common validation approaches: 1) Predictive validity: - How well does the metric predict future team performance when aggregating player values? - Or how well does it predict future plus-minus? 2) Concurrent validity: - Correlation with other respected measures (multi-year RAPM, lineup-adjusted impact). - Not perfect (can become circular), but useful. 3) Face validity / film sanity checks: - Does the top/bottom list pass a “does this make sense” check given role and observed impact? 4) Stability and noise: - Year-to-year correlation for the same player (after controlling for aging). - Sensitivity to minutes and lineup changes. 5) Decomposition accuracy: - If a metric has offense/defense splits, do those align with independent signals (team offense/defense with player on, matchup data, etc.)? PER does okay on stability because box-score rates are stable. It’s less strong on predicting team success compared to impact models.
If PER is flawed, why is it still used?

Because it’s: - Easy to compute and understand at a glance. - Available historically and across eras. - Reasonably correlated with “star-ness” and box-score dominance. - A decent first-pass filter when you don’t have play-by-play or tracking. Also, it’s sticky: once a metric becomes common language, it persists even if better tools exist. The key is using it appropriately—don’t treat it as the definitive ranking, treat it as one descriptive lens.
Let’s talk about “usage.” How does usage interact with these metrics?

Usage rate (USG%) estimates the share of team possessions a player “uses” while on the floor via FGA, FTA, and turnovers. It’s central because: - PER: tends to increase with usage if efficiency holds, because more possessions create more box-score events. But if efficiency drops or turnovers rise, PER can fall. - WS: offensive WS rewards efficient scoring and playmaking; high usage with average efficiency may not yield elite WS/48. - BPM: tries to separate “empty usage” from “impact usage” by fitting to plus-minus; still, high-usage players often rate well because they drive offense. - Impact metrics: high usage can be valuable if it sustains offense against good defenses; but impact metrics can penalize if it reduces team efficiency. In analysis, you often want to look at usage and efficiency together: a high-usage player with +5 rTS is very different from high usage at -2 rTS.
You mentioned rTS. How do you compute it?

rTS is “relative true shooting”: player TS% minus league average TS% for that season (sometimes adjusted for position, but usually just league). TS% = Points / (2 * (FGA + 0.44 * FTA)) Then: rTS = TS% - league_TS% It’s useful because league efficiency changes over time. A 58% TS could be elite in one era and closer to average in another.
PER doesn’t use TS% directly, but it penalizes misses. Is that basically the same?

Related but not the same. TS% compresses scoring efficiency into one number accounting for twos, threes, and free throws. PER’s scoring component is more granular: it separately credits made shots and free throws and separately penalizes misses, with some extra weight for threes. In many typical cases, higher TS% will improve PER. But TS% is purely about scoring efficiency; PER also mixes in rebounds, assists, steals, blocks, turnovers, and fouls. So two players with identical TS% can have very different PER based on other box-score contributions.
How do you feel about “one-number” metrics in general?

They’re useful as an index, but dangerous as a conclusion. The game is multidimensional: offense, defense, creation, scalability, lineup fit. A single scalar can’t fully represent that without strong assumptions. Best practice: - Use one-number metrics to find candidates and outliers. - Then look at a “dashboard” of components (efficiency, volume, turnovers, playmaking, defensive signals, on/off). - And consider role: a player’s value can be in doing low-variance connector tasks that don’t pop in any one-number stat.
Speaking of dashboards, what’s your go-to minimal set?

Minimal but informative set: Offense: - Usage (USG%) - TS% and rTS - Assist% (AST%) - Turnover% (TOV%) - 3P rate (3PA/FGA) and FT rate (FTA/FGA) Possession/role: - Minutes - On-court offensive rating (if available) Defense proxies + context: - Defensive rebound% (DRB%) - Steal% (STL%) - Block% (BLK%) - On-court defensive rating (if available) Impact summary: - BPM (and OBPM/DBPM) - One impact metric like EPM or multi-year RAPM if you can get it - WS and WS/48 for historical continuity That’s enough to avoid being fooled by any single stat.
You didn’t include PER there.

Not because it’s useless—more because BPM and WS overlap its niche while connecting better to impact or wins frameworks. If you have PER, it can still be a quick “box-score rate” reference, especially for older seasons or quick comparisons. But if you’re limiting space, I’d rather keep BPM/VORP and WS/WS48.
Let’s get nerdy: is PER linear? And does that matter?

PER is essentially linear in the box-score inputs (a weighted sum) before normalization and scaling. The pace adjustment and normalization are multiplicative constants, so the core is linear. Does it matter? Yes: - Linearity assumes each additional event has constant marginal value regardless of context. But in reality, marginal value can depend on lineup, opponent, and game state. - It also can’t model interactions (e.g., assists are more valuable when paired with elite finishers; spacing changes the value of drives; etc.). Nonlinear models (or impact models) can implicitly capture some interactions through the data, but they lose interpretability.
Is there any way to “fix” PER without abandoning box score?

You can improve it, but you’ll hit a ceiling. Possible fixes: 1) Refit weights using regression to plus-minus (basically move toward BPM). 2) Add position/role adjustments. 3) Use per-possession rather than per-minute scaling and incorporate team context. 4) Separate components more cleanly: scoring efficiency (TS), possession usage, playmaking, rebounding, defensive events—then combine with empirically validated weights. 5) Introduce priors for defense based on position and team defense (still imperfect). At that point, you’ve created a new metric. The reason PER persists is simplicity and historical inertia, not because it’s the best endpoint.
How do these metrics handle pace? You said PER adjusts for pace. Does BPM?

BPM is expressed per 100 possessions (a plus-minus rate), so it’s naturally pace-neutral in principle. It uses box-score rates and team context to estimate that per-possession impact. WS uses offensive/defensive ratings and possessions in its underlying team win model, so pace is handled through the efficiency framework. WS itself is cumulative and scales with minutes, but the conversion from points to wins is based on efficiency, not raw totals. PER explicitly adjusts per-minute production by pace factors, because per-minute stats are affected by pace.
A lot of fans use per-game stats. Why is per-100 better?

Per-game confounds player production with pace and minutes. If a team plays faster, everyone gets more possessions and thus more counting stats per game. Per-100 possessions (or per-75) normalizes to opportunity. It answers: “If this player played in a 100-possession game, what would their output look like?” That makes cross-team and cross-era comparisons cleaner. You still need efficiency (TS, turnover rate) because volume per-100 can be empty if inefficient.
What about per-36?

Per-36 is a minutes normalization, not a possessions normalization. It helps compare players with different minutes, but it still inherits pace differences. Also, it assumes a player’s per-minute production scales linearly with minutes, which often fails for high-usage roles due to fatigue, matchup changes, and rotation context (even without discussing anything physical, the strategic and substitution context changes). Per-75 possessions is often better than per-36 because it ties to possessions, and it’s closer to “starter workload” without implying linear minute scaling.
Let’s do a thought experiment. Two players: A: 28% usage, 62% TS, 8% TOV, 25% AST B: 22% usage, 58% TS, 12% TOV, 12% AST Who tends to look better in PER, WS/48, BPM?

Without exact box-score lines, we can infer tendencies: - PER: Player A likely crushes it. High usage with elite TS and low turnovers is exactly what PER rewards, plus high assists. Player B is decent but less involved and less efficient with more turnovers. - WS/48: Also favors A strongly, because WS/48 loves efficient scoring and low turnovers; the high AST% helps too. B’s higher turnover rate and lower efficiency drag down offensive WS/48. - BPM: Likely A again, because those inputs correlate with offensive impact. BPM will also consider rebounds and position, so if B is providing something else (rebounding, defense proxies), it could narrow the gap. But purely from these offensive indicators, A is a plus-minus engine archetype. The interesting cases are when A’s role comes with defensive negatives or lineup effects that box-score models miss; then impact metrics could be less enthusiastic. But given A’s efficiency and ball security, it’s hard for impact metrics to not like them.
That makes sense. Now, what’s the cleanest explanation of why defensive box-score stats are so weak?

Because defense is mostly about preventing actions, steering decisions, and coordinating space—events that don’t create a discrete “credit” in the box score. - A block is a visible event, but many great defensive possessions end in a contested miss without a block. - Steals are valuable but sporadic; gambling can inflate steals while harming the defense elsewhere. - Defensive rebounds often depend on scheme and who is tasked to rebound. - Fouls are recorded, but “not fouling” isn’t a recorded positive. So defensive box-score stats are incomplete and can be misleading as proxies for overall defensive impact.
How do impact metrics separate offense and defense then?

They typically use play-by-play scoring margins and attribute changes to players, sometimes with separate modeling for possessions ending in offense/defense. Common approach: - Use adjusted plus-minus for total impact. - Split into offensive and defensive by modeling points scored by the player’s team (offense) and points allowed (defense) during their minutes, again adjusting for teammates/opponents. But the split is noisier than total impact because you’re dividing the signal. Many models stabilize the split using priors from box score and tracking.
Are there any box-score-only defensive metrics you actually trust?

“Trust” is strong, but some are useful as partial signals: - Steal rate and block rate: indicate playmaking and rim protection tendencies. - Defensive rebound rate: indicates involvement on the glass, though scheme matters. - Foul rate: can indicate discipline (but also role; some roles contest more). - Opponent FG% at rim or matchup data would be better, but that’s not box score. As a box-score-only summary, DBPM is okay as a rough estimate, but it’s not something I’d use to make fine distinctions.
How should a stat-head talk about a player without over-relying on one-number metrics?

Use metrics as claims with uncertainty and specify what they measure. Example framing: - “In box-score terms, he’s an elite per-minute producer (PER), and the efficiency is real (rTS, low TOV%).” - “But the impact models are more modest (EPM), suggesting some of the box-score value may not translate one-for-one to lineup scoring margin.” - “His defensive value is hard to capture in box score; on-court defensive rating and multi-year RAPM are better guides, though still noisy.” That communicates a model-based view rather than a single ranking.
I want to understand the relationship between WS and BPM. They sometimes disagree a lot. Why?

They’re optimizing different targets. - WS is an allocation of team wins based on team efficiency and box-score contributions. It’s anchored to team success and minutes. Defensive WS, in particular, heavily leans on team defense. - BPM is an estimate of individual plus-minus per 100 possessions from box score, designed to approximate impact independent of team record (in theory). Disagreement drivers: 1) Team context: a player on a great defensive team can get high DWS even if BPM’s defensive estimate is modest. 2) Minutes: WS totals reward minutes; BPM is a rate. A high-minute player can have big WS with only good BPM. 3) Role: BPM may reward certain playmaking/spacing proxies; WS may reward efficient scoring and team context differently. 4) Position adjustments: BPM uses them; WS is less explicit. So disagreements are informative: they’re telling you which assumptions matter for that player.
If you had to pick one stat to summarize a season historically, what would it be?

If forced to pick one that’s widely available historically and at least tries to connect to winning, I’d pick WS (or WS/48 depending on whether you mean total value or rate). It’s not perfect, but it’s anchored to team wins and is available across long time spans. If you allow more modern data and want closer-to-impact: a well-constructed EPM or multi-year RAPM-based value metric is better. But historically, you often don’t have the inputs.
WS gets criticized for defense allocation. Is there an alternative “wins” metric?

Yes: - “Wins Above Replacement” (WAR) style metrics built from impact models (e.g., RAPM/EPM-based WAR). These convert estimated plus-minus into wins and apply a replacement baseline. - Some public sites provide “RAPTOR WAR” historically (FiveThirtyEight’s model; note it has undergone changes and is no longer updated publicly in the same way), and other communities provide impact-to-wins conversions. These tend to be better at capturing defense and off-ball value, but they depend on model quality and can be less stable in small samples.
What’s the best way to convert plus-minus per 100 into wins?

Roughly: 1) Convert per-100 impact to total points: - Total points added ≈ (Impact per 100 possessions) * (Player possessions played / 100) - Possessions played can be estimated from minutes and team pace. 2) Convert points to wins: - Wins added ≈ points added / (points per win) - Points per win is often around 30, but it varies by season. You can estimate it from league scoring variance and win distribution, or use empirical fits. 3) Apply replacement baseline: - For “WAR,” subtract the impact of a replacement-level player (e.g., -2 to -3 points per 100 is a common ballpark, depending on definition). This yields a cumulative wins-above-replacement number.
You keep saying “replacement baseline.” How do you define replacement in basketball?

Replacement level is the expected performance of an easily available player—someone you can sign or call up without giving up assets. It’s lower than league average because roster spots include stars and specialists, and replacement is more like the fringe rotation/bench pool. Definitions vary: - Some set replacement at a fixed plus-minus rate below average (e.g., -2.0 or -2.5 per 100). - Others define it empirically based on minutes distribution and the performance of low-minute players. - Some define it relative to “minimum salary” caliber. The choice matters: it changes WAR/VORP scales, especially for average players.
Does VORP use a fixed replacement level?

VORP uses a replacement baseline derived from BPM and a positional framework. It’s designed so that replacement is below average, and then it multiplies by minutes to get cumulative value. Exact details depend on the implementation (Basketball-Reference’s VORP has specific constants and a “BPM replacement level” around -2.0 per 100 possessions, with some positional adjustments). The key is: it’s a box-score-based WAR-like stat, not a pure impact stat.
Could you summarize the main “families” of basketball metrics and where PER/WS sit?

Families: 1) Box-score rate indices: - PER, Game Score - Purpose: summarize box-score productivity per minute/game. 2) Box-score win allocation: - Win Shares (WS, WS/48) - Purpose: allocate team wins based on box score + team efficiency. 3) Box-score impact proxies: - BPM, VORP - Purpose: estimate plus-minus impact from box score. 4) On/off and adjusted plus-minus: - Raw on/off, RAPM - Purpose: infer impact from lineup scoring margin, adjusted for context. 5) Hybrid impact models: - EPM, some versions of RPM, other public/private models - Purpose: combine plus-minus data with box-score/tracking priors for stability and accuracy. PER sits in (1). WS sits in (2). They’re older, widely cited, and useful as references, but many analysts prefer (3)-(5) for “impact.”
Last thing: if I see a player with 20 PER, .200 WS/48, and +6 BPM, is that redundant confirmation?

It’s a strong triangulation signal, yes. Those three come from different frameworks: - PER: box-score rate dominance - WS/48: win contribution rate anchored to team efficiency - BPM: estimated per-100 impact from box score If all three are high, it usually means the player is both productive and efficient in the box score in ways that historically correlate with winning. You’d still want to check: - defensive impact via on-court defense or impact models, - playoff translation is a separate question (different opponents and game planning), but as a regular-season summary, that’s a robust “this is an elite season” profile.
Good. Now can we do a deeper dive into why PER uses league normalization to 15 instead of a natural scale?

The normalization to 15 is mainly for interpretability and comparability within a season. A natural-scale metric might drift upward or downward as the league environment changes (pace, scoring, stat distributions). By forcing league average to 15 each year, you get a stable reference point: 15 is average, 20 is clearly above average, 25 is superstar territory. Downside: - It can obscure whether the league overall is becoming more efficient or whether box-score distributions are changing. - It can make cross-era comparisons feel more direct than they really are, because “25 PER” is always “far above average,” but the underlying meaning of “far above average” can shift with how the game is played. So 15 is a branding/communication choice as much as a modeling choice.
That was exactly the kind of explanation I wanted.
Can you explain how WAR is calculated? And why do fWAR and bWAR give different numbers for the same player?

WAR (Wins Above Replacement) is an attempt to summarize a player’s total value in one number expressed as “wins” relative to a readily available “replacement-level” player (think: Triple-A call-up / waiver-wire level). At a high level, all WAR systems do the same conceptual steps: 1) Estimate runs the player contributed with batting (and baserunning, and avoiding double plays, etc.). 2) Estimate runs the player contributed or cost with defense (positioning/fielding and sometimes catcher framing). 3) Add a positional adjustment (harder defensive positions get extra credit because average offense there is lower). 4) Add a replacement-level adjustment (a constant that says even a “replacement” player has some baseline value below average). 5) Convert runs to wins using a runs-per-win factor (roughly ~10 runs = 1 win, but it varies by run environment). 6) For pitchers, use a pitching runs model (either based on runs allowed or FIP-type components). fWAR (FanGraphs) and bWAR (Baseball-Reference) differ mainly in (a) their defensive measurement, (b) their pitching model, and (c) some implementation details like park factors, run estimators, and how they handle multi-position play. So the same player can get different WAR totals. If you want, we can go component-by-component: batting, baserunning, defense, positional, replacement, and then pitchers separately. Do you want position-player WAR first, or pitcher WAR first?
Position-player WAR first. Start from the batting part and be explicit about what “runs” metric is used.

For position players, the batting component is essentially “batting runs above average,” then later converted to wins. The two major public WAR implementations differ in the exact batting runs metric: - fWAR uses wRAA (weighted Runs Above Average), derived from wOBA (weighted On-Base Average). - bWAR uses a runs estimate based on batting runs from linear weights as well, but historically it’s tied to its own “Rbat” built from a version of wOBA/linear weights and adjustments; in practice it’s very similar in spirit, just not identical in coefficients and sometimes in treatment of things like reaching on error or some event classifications. FanGraphs batting: wOBA → wRAA 1) Compute wOBA: wOBA = (Σ (weight_event × count_event)) / (PA - IBB - SF? depends on definition) The numerator uses linear weights for events: BB, HBP, 1B, 2B, 3B, HR (and sometimes IBB separated). The weights are scaled each season to match the run environment (so that wOBA is on an OBP-like scale). 2) Convert wOBA to wRAA: wRAA = ( (wOBA - league_wOBA) / wOBA_scale ) × PA wOBA_scale is a season constant that maps wOBA points to runs per plate appearance. So wRAA is literally “runs above average” from batting, not including baserunning or defense. Baseball-Reference batting: Rbat Baseball-Reference uses a linear weights runs model too. It produces “Batting Runs” (Rbat) relative to average, adjusted for park and league. The exact event weights and scaling are conceptually similar: each offensive event is assigned a run value, summed, and compared to average. Key point: Both are linear-weights batting runs above average. Differences in weights, seasonal constants, park factor application, and event accounting can create small differences. After batting runs above average, both systems add: - Baserunning runs (fWAR: BsR; bWAR: Rbaser) - Double-play avoidance runs (fWAR folds into BsR; bWAR has Rdp) Then defense and position and replacement. If you want an explicit formula for total position-player WAR in “runs” before wins, one can write: Runs Above Replacement (RAR) = BattingRuns + BaserunningRuns + FieldingRuns + PositionalAdjustment + ReplacementRuns Then WAR = RAR / RunsPerWin But each term is estimated differently between systems.
Okay, now baserunning. What exactly is included in BsR? And does bWAR do it differently?

FanGraphs baserunning is typically summarized as BsR, which is a composite of several components: 1) UBR (Ultimate Base Running): takes all non-stolen-base baserunning events and assigns run values based on the base/out state and what the runner did relative to average. Examples: taking an extra base on a single (first-to-third), scoring from second on a single, tagging up on fly balls, advancing on groundouts, etc. It’s essentially context-based (uses run expectancy tables). 2) wSB: a linear weights stolen base runs measure: wSB = (SB × run_value_SB) + (CS × run_value_CS) - (league_rate_adjustment × (SB+CS)) The exact formula is often presented as “stolen base runs above average,” accounting for the fact that simply attempting steals has an average value; you get credit for being better than average. 3) (Historically) something like “wGDP” or running into double plays might be separated, but in FanGraphs, avoiding double plays (wGDP) has been its own line item at times. In current common WAR breakdowns, you’ll see: - BsR (includes UBR + wSB + wGDP in many presentations) - Or BsR plus a separate “GDP” component depending on the page/version. So FanGraphs baserunning is a mix: stolen-base value (mostly linear weights) plus advancement decisions (run expectancy). Baseball-Reference baserunning: bWAR includes: - Rbaser: baserunning runs (similar in spirit to UBR + SB value, built from play-by-play) - Rdp: runs from grounding into double plays relative to average (a separate component) So conceptually similar, but separated differently and derived from B-Ref’s own play-by-play run expectancy framework. Differences come from: - Different run expectancy tables (they’re close but not identical). - Different classification of plays (e.g., fielder’s choice, errors, advancement on outs). - How they attribute credit/blame when multiple runners advance or when throws allow extra bases. Net: BsR and (Rbaser + Rdp) are aiming at the same thing, but their exact values can differ by a run or two (or more for extreme baserunners), which translates to a few tenths of WAR.
Defense is where I hear the biggest differences. Can you compare the defensive components and how they flow into WAR?

Defense is a major divergence. FanGraphs position-player WAR uses: - UZR (Ultimate Zone Rating) as the default public defensive metric for most seasons (with some exceptions/updates). - For catchers, they incorporate framing (FRM) and other catcher defense components depending on era. - Sometimes they use a blend or alternative like OAA-based components in newer contexts for certain displays, but the canonical fWAR for many years has been UZR-based. Baseball-Reference position-player WAR uses: - DRS (Defensive Runs Saved) as the main fielding metric for many modern seasons. - For older seasons, it uses Total Zone (TZ) because detailed play-by-play/zone data is limited historically. - Catcher defense includes components like stolen base/caught stealing run values and, in newer times, framing adjustments depending on their implementation (B-Ref added framing relatively late compared to some others, and the exact incorporation can vary by season). What are UZR and DRS? - Both are “play-by-play” defensive systems that estimate how many runs a fielder saved relative to average by making plays at a certain rate on balls hit to certain locations at certain speeds/types. - They differ in data sources, binning, and modeling. - DRS includes more “components” (e.g., outfield arm runs, double play runs, bunt defense, robbery of hits, etc.) with its own proprietary methods. - UZR also has components (range, errors, arm for OF, double play for IF) but implemented differently. How defense flows into WAR: - Both produce “fielding runs above average” (positive for good defense, negative for bad). - That defensive runs figure is added to batting/baserunning and then combined with positional adjustment and replacement runs. So if one system thinks a shortstop is +15 runs and another thinks he’s +5, that’s a 10-run gap, roughly 1 WAR. That’s why defensive differences can swing WAR a lot. Also important: defensive metrics are noisy year-to-year. Both sites recommend multi-year samples to infer true talent. So a single-season gap might be more “measurement variance” than real disagreement.
Positional adjustment and replacement level—can you give actual typical values and how they’re applied?

Positional adjustment is a run value per full season (usually per 1350 defensive innings, roughly a full-time position-player season in the field). The idea: playing a harder defensive position has opportunity cost; average offense is lower there, so a player who can field that spot is more valuable. Typical FanGraphs positional adjustments per 1350 innings (approximate; exact numbers can vary slightly by year/implementation): - C: +12.5 runs - SS: +7.5 - 2B: +2.5 - 3B: +2.5 - CF: +2.5 - LF: -7.5 - RF: -7.5 - 1B: -12.5 - DH: -17.5 Baseball-Reference uses a very similar positional adjustment structure, though the exact constants may differ slightly, and the way they prorate for innings at each position is similar: if you played 700 innings at SS and 650 at 3B, you get a weighted positional adjustment based on those innings. How applied: PosAdj_runs = Σ (innings_at_pos / 1350) × PosAdj_constant(pos) Replacement level: Replacement runs is a constant added based on playing time, representing that replacement-level is below average. A common rule of thumb: replacement is about 20 runs below average per 600 PA for hitters (and scaled accordingly). For position players, FanGraphs often uses something like: ReplacementRuns ≈ (PA / 600) × 20 But exact constants are calibrated to league totals and the desired distribution of WAR. So a player with 600 PA gets +~20 runs of “replacement credit.” That seems counterintuitive until you remember WAR is “above replacement,” not “above average.” You start from above average components (batting, fielding, baserunning, position) then add replacement to shift the baseline down. Finally: RAR = Batting + Baserunning + Fielding + PosAdj + Replacement WAR = RAR / RunsPerWin RunsPerWin depends on run environment; often around 9.5–10.5.
Can you walk me through a numeric example for a hypothetical player and show how small differences in defense or baserunning could change WAR?

Sure. Suppose Player X has 650 PA, plays mostly CF (1300 innings), and we estimate: FanGraphs-like components (runs): - Batting (wRAA): +25 - Baserunning (BsR): +3 - Fielding (UZR): +8 - Positional (CF): +2.5 × (1300/1350) ≈ +2.4 - Replacement: +20 × (650/600) ≈ +21.7 Total RAR = 25 + 3 + 8 + 2.4 + 21.7 = 60.1 runs Assume RunsPerWin = 10.0 → WAR = 6.0 Now suppose bWAR uses slightly different inputs: - Batting (Rbat): +24 (close) - Baserunning (Rbaser + Rdp): +1 (a bit lower) - Fielding (DRS): +2 (big difference: +6 runs relative to UZR) - Positional: maybe +2.0 (slightly different constant/pro-rating) - Replacement: +22.0 (small difference in replacement scale) RAR = 24 + 1 + 2 + 2.0 + 22 = 51 runs RunsPerWin maybe 9.8 → WAR ≈ 5.2 So even with nearly identical batting, a 6-run defensive difference and 2-run baserunning difference is 8 runs, and then small differences elsewhere can turn 6.0 into ~5.2. That’s not “one is wrong”; it reflects model and measurement uncertainty. If the player is a corner outfielder with negative defensive estimates in one system and neutral in another, the swing can be even larger.
Now pitchers. I know fWAR uses FIP-based WAR and bWAR uses runs allowed. Can you explain both and why that matters?

Pitcher WAR is where the philosophical split is most explicit. FanGraphs pitcher WAR (fWAR): FIP-based - It uses FIP (Fielding Independent Pitching) or a FIP-like model as the core value metric. - FIP estimates runs allowed based on events mostly controlled by the pitcher: strikeouts, walks, hit-by-pitch, home runs (and sometimes constants). FIP = (13*HR + 3*(BB+HBP) - 2*K) / IP + constant - Then FIP is compared to league-average FIP, adjusted for park, and converted to runs and then wins, with an innings-based replacement level. Interpretation: fWAR is closer to “what should have happened given K/BB/HR,” treating balls in play and sequencing as mostly not pitcher skill (or at least not reliably measured year-to-year). Baseball-Reference pitcher WAR (bWAR): RA9-based (runs allowed) - It uses actual runs allowed per nine innings (RA9), adjusted for park and defense, comparing to league average and replacement. - It incorporates earned and unearned runs (or sometimes runs allowed; the exact handling is RA9). - There is an adjustment for team defense behind the pitcher, so a pitcher on a great defense gets some runs shifted away from him. Interpretation: bWAR is closer to “what actually happened (runs on the scoreboard), adjusted for context.” Why it matters: - A pitcher with a low ERA/RA9 but mediocre FIP (e.g., low BABIP, high strand rate) will look better in bWAR than fWAR. - A pitcher with a high ERA but strong FIP (bad defense, bad luck, sequencing) will look better in fWAR. So differences can be large, especially in single seasons. Over multiple seasons, they often converge somewhat, but persistent differences can remain for pitchers with real contact-management skill (if you believe it exists and is stable) or pitchers in extreme contexts.
How does each handle park factors and league context? Are park adjustments applied to batting and pitching similarly?

Both apply park adjustments, but the details differ. For hitters: - Park factors attempt to estimate how a park affects run scoring and/or specific events (HR, doubles, etc.). - FanGraphs often uses multi-year regressed park factors and applies them to wOBA-based batting runs and sometimes to components (HR factor, etc., depending on the metric). - Baseball-Reference also uses park factors and applies them to its batting runs model. Differences arise from: - Data window (1-year vs multi-year, regression strength). - Whether they use overall run park factor or component factors. - How they handle interleague, schedule imbalance, and changes to parks over time. For pitchers: - fWAR: FIP is adjusted for park via HR factor and/or overall factor (because HR is in the formula). FanGraphs provides “FIP-” which is park-adjusted and league-adjusted. - bWAR: RA9 is adjusted for park and then also adjusted for defense. The park factor affects how many runs are expected in that environment. League context: - Both normalize to league average each season, so a 120 wRC+ in a low-scoring era reflects that context. - Runs-per-win also changes with run environment; lower scoring means fewer runs per win. Net: Park and league adjustments are conceptually similar but implemented with different constants and sometimes different factor definitions, which can shift WAR by a few tenths.
You mentioned runs-per-win varies. How is that computed? Is it just 10, or something more careful?

It’s more careful than a fixed 10, though 10 is a decent approximation. Runs per win is tied to the relationship between run differential and winning percentage, often modeled with the Pythagorean expectation: Win% ≈ RS^γ / (RS^γ + RA^γ) where γ is around 1.8–2.0 depending on era. From this, you can derive how many marginal runs correspond to a marginal win at the league scoring level. In a high-scoring environment, it takes more runs to swing a win because variance is larger; in low-scoring, fewer runs. WAR implementations use season-level constants derived from league runs per game: RunsPerWin ≈ (some function of R/G) Commonly it ends up around 9 to 11. For example: - Dead-ball-ish low run scoring: maybe ~9 - High offense era: maybe ~10.5 or ~11 They compute it so that when you sum WAR across a league, it roughly matches the league’s wins above replacement (which is tied to the number of games and the replacement baseline).
What exactly is “replacement level” for pitchers? Is it the same as for hitters?

Replacement level is defined differently for pitchers because playing time is in innings, not plate appearances, and the distribution of pitcher performance differs. Conceptually, replacement level is the expected performance of a readily available pitcher (call-up, waiver claim, minor league depth). In WAR, pitcher replacement is often set as a runs allowed level worse than league average by some fixed amount per 9 innings, or equivalently a win% baseline. FanGraphs fWAR (FIP-based) sets a replacement-level FIP (or FIP- baseline) such that replacement is worse than average by a fixed number of runs per 9, often around +1.0 to +1.5 runs per 9 relative to average (the exact number can change with calibration). Then: RAR_pitch = ( (lgFIP - pitcherFIP) / scale ) × IP + replacement_runs Or equivalently compute runs above replacement directly. Baseball-Reference bWAR (RA9-based) sets replacement level in terms of RA9 (adjusted) and uses innings pitched to scale value. So it’s the same idea—baseline below average—but the numeric baseline differs and is calibrated to the league’s distribution of pitcher innings and performance.
If fWAR is FIP-based, does it ignore pitcher ability to suppress hits on balls in play? And is that a flaw?

fWAR largely treats outcomes on balls in play (BABIP) and sequencing (strand rate) as not reliably attributable to pitcher skill in a single season, so it doesn’t give much credit for “hit suppression” beyond what’s captured by strikeouts, walks, and home runs. Is that a flaw? Depends on the purpose: - If you want an estimate of “true talent” or forward-looking value, FIP-based metrics often outperform ERA in prediction because K/BB/HR stabilize faster and are more pitcher-controlled. - If you want “credit for what happened,” ignoring BABIP/strand rate can feel wrong, especially for awards or historical accounting. There’s also evidence that some pitchers have persistent contact-management skill (quality of contact, inducing weak contact, etc.), but it’s complicated: - Traditional FIP doesn’t include exit velocity/launch angle. - Modern approaches like Statcast-based ERA estimators (xERA) or models that include hard-hit rates try to capture that. - Even then, separating pitcher skill from defense, park, and randomness is hard. So fWAR is not “ignoring reality” so much as choosing a model emphasizing repeatable components.
For position players, is there an analogous split between “what happened” and “what should have happened,” like with pitchers?

Less stark, because batting linear weights already credits actual outcomes (singles, doubles, etc.). There are expected stats (xwOBA) that model “should have happened” based on quality of contact, but WAR implementations generally use actual results for offense. Defense is closer to the pitcher split in a sense: - Defensive metrics are estimates with uncertainty; they try to measure what happened (outs made relative to average on batted-ball opportunities), but the measurement is noisy and model-dependent. - There isn’t a single “fielding independent” analog that is widely accepted for defense; we’re stuck with observational models. So for hitters: - Offense: mostly “what happened” (results-based). - Defense: modeled, noisy, and can differ across systems. - Baserunning: mostly “what happened” (advancement decisions and SB/CS outcomes), with some modeling.
How do these systems handle playing time and rate vs counting? Like, can a player with mediocre rate stats still have high WAR?

Yes. WAR is additive with playing time because it’s based on total runs contributed, not rate alone. A player with: - Slightly above-average hitting - Average defense - Full-time playing time (700 PA) can accumulate 3–4 WAR just by being solid and playing every day. Conversely, a part-time player with excellent rate stats can have lower WAR because fewer PA means fewer runs accumulated. This is by design: a 3-WAR player is worth about three wins over replacement across the season, which depends on both quality and quantity. WAR also implicitly includes durability and usage: - For position players, more PA and innings means more opportunities to add value. - For pitchers, more IP matters a lot; a great reliever in 60 IP can’t match a great starter in 200 IP unless the per-inning dominance is extreme.
Relievers—how do fWAR and bWAR treat them? And what about leverage?

Reliever WAR is tricky because relievers pitch fewer innings but in higher leverage situations. Core approaches: - fWAR uses FIP-based runs above replacement, scaled by innings, with a reliever replacement level and often a leverage adjustment in a separate stat (like WPA/LI), but not as a core part of WAR. FanGraphs also has “RA9-WAR” as an alternative that’s RA9-based and may be used by some analysts. - bWAR uses RA9-based runs allowed, adjusted for park and defense, scaled by innings, with a reliever replacement baseline. Leverage: - Standard WAR generally does not include leverage as extra credit. It values a run prevented the same regardless of inning/score, because it’s trying to measure “true value” independent of context and because teams can (to some extent) choose how to deploy relievers. - Context-dependent metrics like WPA (Win Probability Added) explicitly include leverage and game situation. - There’s also “WPA/LI” which tries to strip out leverage by dividing by leverage index, giving a context-neutral measure of win probability contribution. So a shutdown closer may have modest WAR but huge WPA if he consistently pitches high-leverage innings successfully. WAR says: the same performance in lower leverage is equally “valuable” in a context-neutral sense; WPA says: it mattered more in actual wins.
You mentioned catcher framing. How does that enter WAR, and is it consistent across sources?

Catcher framing assigns run value to how a catcher influences called strikes vs balls. A catcher who gets extra strikes reduces walks and increases strikeouts (or at least improves counts), which has run value. In WAR frameworks: - Framing runs are treated like a defensive component for catchers and added to fielding runs. - FanGraphs incorporated framing earlier and more prominently, using publicly available framing metrics (e.g., from Baseball Prospectus originally, then their own/Statcast-era sources). - Baseball-Reference added framing later; their catching defense includes multiple components (stolen base control, blocking, framing), but the exact seasons covered and magnitude can differ. This can create big discrepancies for catchers, because framing can be worth on the order of ±10 to ±20 runs in a season for extremes, which is 1–2 WAR. Also, framing measurement has evolved: - Different pitch tracking systems, strike zone models, and umpire behavior adjustments. - Statcast era improved pitch location accuracy, changing framing estimates. So catcher WAR can vary notably depending on framing model and inclusion.
How should I interpret a difference like 4.5 fWAR vs 6.0 bWAR for a pitcher? Is one “right”?

Treat it as a range reflecting different assumptions. If a pitcher has: - Low runs allowed (RA9/ERA) but higher FIP (fewer Ks, more BBs/HRs, or vice versa) then bWAR will be higher because it credits the actual run prevention, while fWAR will be lower because it thinks some of that is not attributable to the pitcher’s core skills. Neither is universally “right.” It depends on what question you’re asking: - “How valuable was he in terms of actual wins this season?” bWAR aligns more with that (though still modeled via defense/park adjustments). - “How good was his underlying performance independent of defense/variance?” fWAR aligns more. Also, both have uncertainty: - Defense adjustments for pitchers are imperfect. - Sequencing and strand rate have some pitcher influence (e.g., certain pitch arsenals may play up with runners on). - Park factors and scorer decisions matter. So it’s best to look at component stats: ERA-, FIP-, xFIP-, SIERA, innings, and then see why they diverge.
For hitters, if I see fWAR 5.2 and bWAR 3.8, what are the likely drivers?

Usually defense and positional/playing-time splits. Common drivers: 1) Defensive metric disagreement (UZR vs DRS/TZ). A 15-run swing in defense is 1.5 WAR. 2) Different treatment of positional adjustment for multi-position players. If one credits more innings at SS vs 2B, it changes. 3) Park factor differences for offense (usually smaller, but can matter). 4) Baserunning differences (typically smaller than defense, but can add up). 5) Catcher framing inclusion differences (big for catchers). So I’d check: - fWAR: look at UZR (or DEF), positional adjustment, BsR. - bWAR: look at fielding runs, positional runs, baserunning and DP runs. If the gap is big, it’s almost always defense.
Can you explain how “league average” is defined in these metrics? Is it per PA, per out, per game?

It depends on the component: Batting: - wRAA is computed per PA relative to league average wOBA, then multiplied by PA. So league average is per PA. Baserunning: - UBR is per baserunning opportunity relative to average. Ultimately it’s in runs, aggregated over opportunities. Defense: - UZR/DRS are relative to average for that position, based on opportunities (balls in zone, etc.). So “average” is per opportunity at that position. Positional adjustment: - Defined as a constant per inning (or per 1350 innings) to represent average position difficulty. Replacement: - Defined per PA or per inning pitched. Runs per win: - Derived from league scoring per game. So “average” is not one universal baseline; each component has its own notion of average, but they’re all converted into runs on a common scale, then aggregated.
I want to understand the conversion from wOBA to runs more deeply. What is wOBA scale, and why is it needed?

wOBA is designed to look like OBP in scale (around .320–.340 typical), but its numerator is linear weights in “run” units scaled to that OBP-like range. To convert a difference in wOBA into runs, you need a scale factor. The wOBA scale is a season constant that ensures: wRAA = (wOBA - lg_wOBA) / wOBA_scale × PA produces run values consistent with the league’s run scoring. Intuition: - If wOBA were literally “runs per PA,” you could multiply by PA directly. But it isn’t; it’s scaled. - wOBA_scale maps “points of wOBA” to “runs per PA.” It’s computed from the underlying linear weights and run environment. In practice, FanGraphs publishes wOBA weights and wOBA scale each year. Example: If wOBA_scale is ~1.25 and a player is .350 wOBA in a league with .320, difference is .030. Runs above average per PA = .030 / 1.25 = .024 runs/PA Over 600 PA: ~14.4 runs That’s the batting runs above average.
How do linear weights get determined? Are they fixed?

They’re derived empirically from run expectancy. Method sketch: 1) Use play-by-play data to compute the average change in run expectancy (RE) for each event type (BB, 1B, 2B, etc.) given base/out states. 2) Average those RE changes across all occurrences of that event in the season (or multi-year sample). 3) Those averages are the linear weights in “runs” for each event. Because run environments change (e.g., home run rates, overall scoring), the weights can shift slightly year to year. For example, the marginal value of a home run versus a double depends on how often runners are on base and how many runs are typically scored. So they’re not fixed constants forever; they’re recalibrated periodically (often annually) to match the season’s environment. wOBA weights are essentially linear weights rescaled to the wOBA scale.
What about the “positional adjustment” numbers—where do those come from?

They’re based on observed offensive differences by position and the idea of scarcity/defensive spectrum. A common approach: 1) Estimate average offensive performance by position (e.g., SS typically hits worse than 1B). 2) The difference is assumed to reflect the defensive demands: teams accept lower offense at SS because defense is harder and more important. 3) Convert those differences into runs and set positional adjustments so that an average defender at each position, with average offense, has comparable overall value after adjustment. There’s also calibration to ensure the overall distribution of value across positions makes sense and that the league totals balance. These adjustments are updated occasionally as positional offense distributions change (e.g., if shortstops hit better league-wide than in past eras, the scarcity might be slightly less, though SS is still a premium spot).
I’ve heard criticism that positional adjustments “double count” defense since defensive metrics already account for difficulty. Is that true?

Not exactly, but it’s a subtle point. Defensive metrics like UZR/DRS measure performance relative to average at that position. They don’t directly compare a SS to a 1B on the same scale of difficulty; they compare each to positional peers. Positional adjustment is meant to put positions on a common value baseline, reflecting that: - Being an average SS is harder (and more valuable) than being an average 1B. - The market shows teams pay more for competent SS/CF/C than for 1B/DH given similar offense, consistent with scarcity. So defensive runs are “within-position,” positional adjustment is “across-position.” Could there be some overlap? Possibly if the defensive metric’s “average” baseline already embeds some difficulty assumptions. But in general, the two pieces serve different purposes: one measures how well you played the position, the other values the position itself.
How do WAR systems treat players who switch positions mid-season? Any pitfalls?

They prorate positional adjustment (and defensive value) by innings at each position. Pitfalls: - Small samples at a position: defensive metrics can be extremely noisy in, say, 200 innings. If a player plays 200 innings at SS and grades poorly, it may not reflect true ability. - Some systems regress defensive metrics for small samples or incorporate prior years; others present raw single-season values. - The positional adjustment might “overcredit” a player for a brief stint at a premium position even if he was moved off it because he couldn’t handle it. But the defensive metric should penalize poor performance, at least in theory. Also, some systems treat the “position played most” as the primary for certain displays, but the underlying WAR uses innings-weighted adjustments.
How about designated hitters? Why is the DH adjustment so negative?

DH gets a large negative positional adjustment (around -17.5 runs per full season) because: - DH provides essentially no defensive value. - A DH “roster spot” is easier to fill; many players can hit-only. - If you can’t field, your team must carry a defender elsewhere, reducing flexibility. So a DH needs to hit significantly better than average to be valuable at the same level as a competent fielder at a position. That’s why great hitters who are DH-only can still have high WAR (because their batting runs are enormous), but average hitters at DH are near replacement.
Let’s talk about the “replacement player” concept. How is replacement level set empirically?

It’s partly empirical, partly convention/calibration. Common calibrations: - Replacement level corresponds to a team winning percentage around .294 (historically used in some frameworks), which is about 48 wins in a 162-game season. That implies replacement is about 2 wins per 10 games below .500, etc. - Another approach: set replacement such that the sum of WAR across all players equals the number of wins above replacement in the league. Since total team wins are fixed, and replacement baseline is defined, you can solve for the constant that makes totals line up. Empirically, you can look at performance of: - Players who are freely available (minor league call-ups, waiver claims, non-tender types). - The “marginal” players getting playing time due to injuries. But selection bias is big: even “replacement” players in MLB are chosen because teams think they’re the best available, and they might be slightly better than the theoretical replacement baseline. So in practice, WAR implementations define replacement level as a constant below average per PA or per inning, tuned to produce reasonable league totals and align with roster realities.
How does WAR handle the fact that teams can choose to play someone? Like, a player with -2 WAR still got playing time.

WAR is descriptive of value given playing time, not a statement that the player “should not have played” in isolation. Reasons a negative-WAR player might play: - Injury replacements and no better options at the moment. - Prospects getting development time. - Contract/roster constraints. - Small sample fluctuations; the player might be better than the observed performance. - Defensive position scarcity; even a weak hitter might be the best available shortstop. Replacement level is an average baseline. Individual “replacement” players can be better or worse; WAR is measuring how this player performed relative to that baseline in that season.
Can you explain the relationship between WAR and wRC+? People often cite both.

wRC+ is an offense-only rate stat: - It measures runs created per plate appearance relative to league average, adjusted for park and league. - 100 is league average; 120 is 20% above average. WAR is total value: - Offense (in runs) + baserunning + defense + position + replacement, converted to wins. Relationship: - A player with high wRC+ but poor defense and a low-value position (1B/DH) might have less WAR than you’d think. - A player with modest wRC+ but elite defense at SS/CF/C can have high WAR. Also, wRC+ is rate-based; WAR is counting-based. Two players can have the same wRC+ but different WAR due to playing time.
If I want to compare players across eras, is WAR good for that? What are its limitations?

WAR is one of the better single-number tools for cross-era comparisons because it’s: - Context-adjusted (league and park). - Based on runs and wins, which are universal currencies. But limitations: 1) Data quality: Defensive metrics for early eras rely on Total Zone or other approximations, less precise than modern play-by-play tracking. 2) Role changes: pitcher usage (complete games vs modern bullpens) changes the replacement baseline and innings distribution; comparing pitcher WAR across eras needs care. 3) Rule changes and scoring: run environment changes affect runs-per-win and event values. 4) Integration and league quality: the talent pool changed drastically over time. WAR doesn’t fully adjust for league-quality differences. 5) Park factor estimation historically is harder. So WAR is useful, but for deep era comparisons, you should supplement with era-adjusted rate stats (OPS+, ERA+), workload context, and qualitative considerations.
For pitchers specifically, what’s the relationship between ERA+, FIP-, and WAR?

ERA+ and FIP- are rate stats: - ERA+ is park- and league-adjusted ERA, scaled so 100 is average, higher is better. - FIP- is park- and league-adjusted FIP, scaled so 100 is average, lower is better (sometimes inverted; FanGraphs uses FIP- where lower is better). WAR for pitchers: - fWAR is derived from FIP (or FIP-), plus innings and replacement. - bWAR is derived from RA9 (similar to ERA but includes unearned runs), plus innings, and adjustments. So: - A pitcher with great ERA+ but mediocre FIP- likely has higher bWAR than fWAR. - Innings matter hugely: a 120 ERA+ in 220 IP can be 6+ WAR; the same in 120 IP is much less. ERA+ and FIP- are often good for quickly seeing why WAR differs: if ERA+ says elite but FIP- says merely good, that points to sequencing/contact/defense effects.
How do park factors affect FIP? Since FIP is HR-heavy, do parks with short porches distort it?

Yes, and that’s why park adjustment matters. Because FIP includes HR with a big coefficient, parks that inflate home runs will inflate unadjusted FIP. FanGraphs’ FIP- (and the WAR process) applies park adjustments so pitchers in HR-friendly parks aren’t overly penalized relative to league context. However, park factor estimation for HR specifically can be noisy: - Weather, roster composition, and schedule can affect observed HR rates. - Parks can change (fences moved). - A pitcher’s home/road mix and opponent mix matters. So park-adjusted FIP is an improvement, but not perfect. Also, some analysts prefer xFIP, which replaces actual HR with expected HR based on fly balls and league-average HR/FB, to reduce noise. FanGraphs has xFIP-based analysis, but fWAR uses FIP, not xFIP, by default.
Is there a “best practice” for using WAR in analysis? Like, if I’m writing a piece comparing MVP candidates.

A solid practice is: 1) Use WAR as a starting point, not the conclusion. 2) Check both fWAR and bWAR (and possibly a third like Baseball Prospectus WARP) to see if there’s consensus or a gap. 3) Decompose: - Offense: wRC+ and batting runs - Defense: UZR/DRS/OAA and positional context - Baserunning: BsR - Playing time: PA/innings 4) Consider uncertainty: - Defense has wide error bars. - Single-season pitcher WAR varies by model. 5) Use context-dependent metrics if relevant: - WPA for “most valuable in actual win probability” - Clutch metrics cautiously (low signal) 6) Make your criteria explicit: Are you rewarding “best player” (talent/performance) or “most valuable” (including context and actual outcomes)?
